speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome to Git Going with GitHub, episode 0: Welcome to Git Going with GitHub. I am Alex. Today we are going to make the shape of the workshop something you can explain, practice, and recover from when the interface surprises you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I will be the voice of the learner who is willing to ask, what is this for, where am I, and how do I know I did it right?

speaker: ALEX
text: The big idea today: A tour of the workshop structure, the two-day arc, and what you will accomplish. We will name the concept, explain why it matters, practice the move, and point out the checks that tell you the work is real.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the episode should work even if someone has not read the chapter yet.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The transcript has to stand on its own. It can point toward practice, but it should teach the concept right here in the conversation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How should they picture the shape of the workshop?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub Learning Room - Your Complete Workshop Companion: the learner is about to begin a two-day journey into open source collaboration using GitHub, VS Code, and GitHub Copilot - all designed for screen reader and keyboard-only navigation. The next useful detail is concrete: This guide is your starting point and table of contents for everything in this workshop.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Here is the learner-facing version. This is a two-day workshop built around one idea: you will make real contributions to a real open source project. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should feel predictable before the first live session starts?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Live Agenda and Self-Paced Curriculum: the live hackathon agenda is intentionally smaller than the full curriculum. That matters in practice: Live sessions prioritize the core contribution path, while the complete chapter set remains available for self-paced preparation, catch-up, remote participation, and post-event continuation.

speaker: ALEX
text: That shows up in the workshop in a few specific ways. Live core: The facilitator chooses the minimum path needed for participants to make and understand a real contribution. Async follow-up: Chapters and challenges not covered live can be completed after the session using the Learning Room, solutions, podcasts, and Slack channel. Remote participation: Remote cohorts should use the same checkpoints and evidence prompts, with written instructions available before each live block.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Anchor this part on Day 1 - GitHub Foundations (Browser). You learn GitHub's web interface using only your keyboard and screen reader. This is the part to say slowly: The live Day 1 core path gets you through repository navigation, issues, branches, commits, and a first pull request.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where is the promise of the workshop, underneath all the logistics?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: you move to Visual Studio Code, learn GitHub Copilot, and activate the Accessibility Agents ecosystem - 55 AI agents across 3 teams and 5 platforms that amplify every skill you built on Day 1. The listener should be able to check this: The live Day 2 core path prepares you to make a real contribution, and the async continuation path gives you time to polish and submit it well.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. Do not treat The Journey Arc as decoration. The key principle: Learn the manual skill first, then see how it is automated. That is not trivia. The agents only make sense when you already understand what they are doing. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the pre-flight check here?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Before You Begin is still useful because start with Get Going with GitHub if you want the most guided path. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: It explains how GitHub Classroom creates your private Learning Room repository, how Challenge 1 appears, how evidence prompts work, and how to choose between browser, github.dev, VS Code, GitHub Desktop, and command-line paths.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. Creating a GitHub account. Installing Git. Setting up VS Code (optional for Day 1, required for Day 2). Configuring your screen reader for GitHub.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Put Companion Audio Series into plain language. The audio track is a standalone teaching companion for the same concepts. The useful version is: Use the episode whenever audio helps you enter or revisit the topic.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. Browse the podcast episodes with HTML5 audio players. Subscribe via RSS in your preferred podcast app. Episodes are 8-18 minutes each - perfect for commutes, walks, or screen reader breaks.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where does the workshop stop being a tour and start becoming contribution?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. These chapters are designed to be read and practiced in order. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: The live Day 1 agenda covers the core path in a shorter Pacific-time event day and treats later challenges as stretch or async follow-up.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before the learner moves on. This part earns its place because day 2 moves you from the browser to the desktop. That gives the learner a foothold: every skill maps directly to what you learned on Day 1. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: Appendices - Reference Material: Open these at any time during the workshop. The next useful detail is concrete: They are not part of the chapter sequence - use them when you need them.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. Here is the learner-facing version. The workshop includes structured exercises across the curriculum. Put another way, every exercise is designed to be completed in 1-5 minutes, is impossible to fail, and follows the same pattern: Try It - You're done when - What success feels like.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What do you want them to do when the plan breaks?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Getting Help: if you get stuck at any point during the workshop, these resources are always available. That matters in practice: Open a support issue at https://github.com/Community-Access/support/issues describing what you tried, what happened, and what you expected.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Anchor this part on Workshop at a Glance. Start with Chapter 00: Pre-Workshop Setup. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: this workshop is designed so you are never left guessing what comes next. The listener should be able to check this: The goal is to build confidence one checkable step at a time.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. A GitHub Classroom assignment link from the facilitator. Your own private Learning Room repository. Challenge issues that tell you exactly what to do. Evidence prompts that tell you what to post when you finish.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat When Tools or Pages Change as decoration. GitHub.com, GitHub Classroom, VS Code, GitHub Copilot, github.dev, browser extensions, and agent tools change often. That is not trivia. This course is actively maintained, and we use official sources and live checks wherever possible, but your screen may not always match the exact wording in a step.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, check the URL and browser tab title. Then, move by headings, landmarks, tabs, and button names instead of relying only on visual order. After that, open keyboard shortcut help (? on GitHub) or the VS Code Command Palette when a shortcut or control differs. Finally, read the surrounding labels before activating a control. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Ask a facilitator or file a curriculum issue with the page link, the step that differed, and what you heard or observed. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does someone need before they touch the keyboard?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Step 1 - Know Your Starting Place is still useful because before Day 1 starts, complete Chapter 00: Pre-Workshop Setup. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: That chapter helps you create or verify your GitHub account, configure accessibility settings, choose a browser, install Git and VS Code, and confirm your screen reader setup.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Put Step 2 - Accept the GitHub Classroom Assignment into plain language. At the start of Day 1, the facilitator gives you a GitHub Classroom assignment link. The useful version is: It usually starts with https://classroom.github.com/a/. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Open the assignment link in the browser where you are signed in to GitHub; If GitHub asks you to authorize GitHub Classroom, activate Authorize GitHub Classroom; If you are asked to choose your name from a roster, find your name and select it. If your name is missing, use the skip option and tell the facilitator; and Activate Accept this assignment. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 3 checks: Wait while GitHub Classroom creates your private repository; Refresh the page until the repository link appears; and Open the repository link and bookmark it. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. The Learning Room is a private repository created from a template. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Everyone starts from the same materials, but your work belongs to you.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. You have your own issues, branches, commits, and pull requests. Other students do not see your work unless the facilitator intentionally pairs you. Mistakes are expected and recoverable. Bot feedback is educational, not punitive.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because after your Learning Room repository is created, the Student Progression Bot creates your first challenge issue. That gives the learner a foothold: when you close a challenge issue, the next challenge opens.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is open your Learning Room repository. Step two is navigate to the Issues tab. On GitHub, the keyboard shortcut is G then I. Step three is find an issue titled Challenge 1: Find Your Way Around. Step four is open the issue and read the body from top to bottom. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Step 4 - Find Challenge 1. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, follow the checklist in the issue. Then, post your evidence in the evidence field or as the requested comment. After that, close the challenge issue when the instructions tell you to close it. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What stays the same when the tool changes?

speaker: ALEX
text: Step 5 - Choose the Tool That Fits the Moment: There is no single correct way to use GitHub. The next useful detail is concrete: The workshop teaches the workflow first, then offers tool paths.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. GitHub.com in the browser: Best for Day 1, issues, pull requests, repository navigation, and reviews. github.dev: Best when you want a VS Code-style editor in the browser without installing anything. Press the period key from many repository pages to open it. VS Code desktop: Best for Day 2, local Git, Copilot, extensions, and deeper editing work. GitHub Desktop: Best if you want a desktop Git workflow without typing Git commands.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Here is the learner-facing version. When you feel lost, listen for structure before you take action. Put another way, if you are not sure where you are, pause and navigate by headings or landmarks. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. Page title. Repository name heading. Landmark names such as main content or repository navigation. Tab names such as Code, Issues, and Pull requests.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do the two days connect instead of feeling like separate courses?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Step 7 - Use the Support Built into the Course: if something does not work, do not start over silently. That matters in practice: Read the latest bot message, check the challenge issue, and ask for help with the link to the page where you are stuck.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. Every challenge issue includes instructions and evidence prompts. Every chapter has an If You Get Stuck section. Every challenge has a reference solution in the solutions folder. Aria posts feedback on pull requests.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Step 8 - Your First Success Check. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Anchor this part on Step 8 - Your First Success Check. You are ready to continue when you can say these four things. This is the part to say slowly: You do not need to understand every GitHub feature before you begin.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: I can open my Learning Room repository; I can find the Issues tab; I can open Challenge 1; and I know where to post my evidence and how to ask for help. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Where to Go Next. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: use this order if you want the gentlest path. The listener should be able to check this: We will keep the path explicit, and we will keep giving you the next step.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is chapter 00: Pre-Workshop Setup. Step two is chapter 01: Choose Your Tools. Step three is chapter 02: Understanding GitHub. Step four is chapter 03: Navigating Repositories. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Where to Go Next, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, chapter 04: The Learning Room. Then, your Challenge 1: Find Your Way Around issue in your Learning Room repository. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 0. Next in the series is episode 1, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
